APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This work addresses a core NLP problem (keyphrase generation) with a novel modeling component (encoder–decoder with copy mechanism) and presents a large training corpus plus a new large test set (KP20k), which is highly relevant to top NLP/ML venues. Methods (bi-GRU encoder, attention, copy, beam search), comparisons to standard baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, KEA, Maui), and standard evaluation metrics (precision/recall/F1) match community expectations. The cross-domain analysis (scientific publications and news) and large-scale empirical gains further position it well for this audience.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important problem (automatic keyphrase generation) and demonstrates strong empirical gains — CopyRNN with a copy mechanism outperforms traditional unsupervised and supervised baselines by >20% on present keyphrase prediction and can generate absent keyphrases. The construction of a very large training corpus (~527k papers) and a new 20k-article test set (KP20k) provides a useful benchmark likely to be widely adopted and enables robust training. While some limitations remain (OOV handling, and benefits from domain-adaptive training for non-publication data), the results are likely to change practice in the subfield and open significant follow-up work on generative keyphrase models and domain adaptation.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies an encoder–decoder RNN with attention plus a copy mechanism (CopyRNN) specifically to keyphrase generation, enabling both present and absent keyphrase prediction and addressing OOV token recovery that pure generative RNNs miss. It also contributes a large training corpus and a new large test set (KP20k), and shows substantial gains over traditional unsupervised/supervised baselines. While the model components (bidirectional GRU, attention, copy) are existing techniques, their non-trivial combination and dataset creation for this task clearly differentiate the work from prior approaches and would inform experts in the field.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments are broad and convincing: the model is trained on a very large scientific corpus (~527K) and evaluated across multiple standard datasets (Inspec, Krapivin, NUS, SemEval-2010) plus a new large test set (KP20k) and a news dataset (DUC-2001), with comparisons to many strong baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank variants, KEA, Maui). The paper reports relevant limitations (copy vs pure generative RNN behavior, 2% OOV issue, ExpandRank time complexity, small Krapivin) and provides hyperparameters, but the summary does not indicate thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., copy vs attention vs encoder variants beyond the pure generative RNN) nor mention code release, leaving some reproducibility and analysis gaps. Related work coverage is described as thorough, supporting the placement of the contributions.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear methodological thread (bidirectional GRU encoder, GRU decoder with attention and copy mechanism, beam search) followed by experiments on multiple datasets (KP20k, Inspec, Krapivin, NUS, SemEval-2010, DUC-2001). The main contributions are stated clearly (CopyRNN with copy mechanism, large training corpus, new KP20k test set) and experiments report standard metrics and thorough baseline comparisons. Notation and experimental details seem consistent (hyperparameters reported) and related work coverage is thorough; overall writing quality is adequate with only minor presentation ambiguities rather than major clarity problems.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s main claims are supported by extensive experiments: a large training corpus (trained on 527,830 articles), a new large test set (KP20k, 20,000 articles), and comparisons against a broad set of supervised and unsupervised baselines showing >20% gains on present keyphrase prediction. The proposed architecture (bi-GRU encoder, GRU decoder with attention and a copy mechanism, beam search) and reported hyperparameters make the experimental methodology sound and appropriate for the task. Minor technical gaps include no mention of statistical significance testing of results and no mathematical proofs (which are not essential here), and code availability is not reported—these are weaknesses but do not materially undermine the experimental claims.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate classic unsupervised (Tf-Idf, TextRank, SingleRank, ExpandRank) and supervised (KEA, Maui) baselines on the same datasets (Inspec, Krapivin, NUS, SemEval-2010, KP20k) using standard precision/recall/F1 metrics, and reports strong gains. Comparisons appear fair and the authors acknowledge when baselines fail or underperform (e.g., ExpandRank’s time complexity on KP20k) and analyze limitations of the pure generative RNN (OOV, overly general phrases) versus the proposed copy mechanism. Minor omissions include no explicit comparison to other contemporary neural sequence models or domain-adaptive baselines for the news experiments, so the evaluation is strong but not fully exhaustive.

main_claims: Proposes a generative deep keyphrase generation model (encoder-decoder) named CopyRNN that incorporates a copy mechanism to better link generated keyphrases to source text.

The CopyRNN model significantly outperforms traditional unsupervised and supervised baselines on present keyphrase prediction (exceeding best baselines by >20% on average) and can generate absent keyphrases based on semantic meaning.
Constructed a large training corpus of scientific metadata (567,830 articles obtained; model trained on 527,830) and a new large test set KP20k (20,000 articles) to evaluate the approach.

Uses a bidirectional GRU encoder, a GRU decoder with attention and a copy mechanism, and beam search for generation; demonstrates applicability across scientific publications and news articles (without domain-adaptive training).
_summary.methodology: Train an RNN encoder-decoder (bidirectional GRU encoder, GRU decoder) with attention and a copy mechanism on title+abstract to generate keyphrases; use beam search to produce top predictions.
datasets: training corpus of 567,830 scientific articles (model trained on 527,830)
limitations: Pure generative RNN (without copy) tends to generate too-general keyphrases and fails to recall keyphrases with out-of-vocabulary words (vocabulary limited to top 50,000).
Nearly 2% of correct predictions contain out-of-vocabulary words (not handled by generative RNN).
ExpandRank fails to return any result on KP20k due to high time complexity.
The Krapivin dataset (2,304 papers) is too small to train a robust recurrent neural network model (necessitating a much larger corpus).
Model trained on publication data was applied to news articles without adaptive training and would likely benefit from domain-specific training.
